Modeling Physiological and Behavioral Predictors of Stress Using a Linear Mixed Model on Wearable Health Data

Using Synthetic Longitudinal Wearable Device Data

Author

Savannah Davis
Bryce Mattos
Jessica Jones
Jordan Gainey
(Advisor: Dr. Cohen)

Published

June 14, 2026

Slides: slides.html

Introduction

The introduction should:

  • Develop a storyline that captures attention and maintains interest.

  • Your audience is your peers

  • Clearly state the problem or question you’re addressing.

  • Introduce why it is relevant needs.

  • Provide an overview of your approach.

Add intro here

Methods

  • Detail the models or algorithms used.

  • Justify your choices based on the problem and data.

The common non-parametric regression model is \(Y_i = m(X_i) + \varepsilon_i\), where \(Y_i\) can be defined as the sum of the regression function value \(m(x)\) for \(X_i\). Here \(m(x)\) is unknown and \(\varepsilon_i\) some errors. With the help of this definition, we can create the estimation for local averaging i.e. \(m(x)\) can be estimated with the product of \(Y_i\) average and \(X_i\) is near to \(x\). In other words, this means that we are discovering the line through the data points with the help of surrounding data points. The estimation formula is printed below:

\[ M_n(x) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} W_n (X_i) Y_i \tag{1} \]\(W_n(x)\) is the sum of weights that belongs to all real numbers. Weights are positive numbers and small if \(X_i\) is far from \(x\).

Another equation:

\[ y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_1 +\varepsilon_i \]

Analysis and Results

Data Exploration and Visualization

  • Describe your data sources and collection process.

  • Present initial findings and insights through visualizations.

  • Highlight unexpected patterns or anomalies.

This report utilizes the Health + Wellness + Stress/Sleep Tracking dataset obtained from Kaggle. The dataset contains wearable health measurements and behavioral measurements for 300 simulated individuals over a 6 month period. Variables include but are not limited to: demographic characteristics, physical activity, sleep metrics, cardiovascular measurements, and stress score. This dataset is longitudinal and is suitable for analyzing physiological and behavioral predictors of stress using a linear mixed model. (Akarsu 2025)

Code 
library(tidyverse)
library(knitr)
library(kableExtra)
library(corrplot)
library(lme4)
library(lmerTest)
library(performance)
library(broom.mixed)
library(car)
Code 
# Load Data

df = read_csv("wearables_health_6mo_daily.csv")
Code 
# Quick overview of dataset

dataset_overview <- tibble(
  Metric = c("Total Observations", "Total Variables", "Unique Users"),
  Value = c(
    scales::comma(nrow(df)),
    scales::comma(ncol(df)),
    scales::comma(n_distinct(df$user_id))
  )
)

dataset_overview %>%
  kable(caption = "Dataset Overview") %>%
  kable_styling(
    bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed"),
    full_width = FALSE
  )
Dataset Overview
Metric Value
Total Observations 55,200
Total Variables 33
Unique Users 300

Descriptive Statistics

Code 
# Function to create descriptive statistics tables

create_descriptive_table <- function(data, variables, caption) {

  data %>%
    select(all_of(variables)) %>%
    pivot_longer(
      cols = everything(),
      names_to = "Variable",
      values_to = "Value"
    ) %>%
    group_by(Variable) %>%
    summarise(
      Mean = round(mean(Value, na.rm = TRUE), 2),
      SD = round(sd(Value, na.rm = TRUE), 2),
      Min = round(min(Value, na.rm = TRUE), 2),
      Median = round(median(Value, na.rm = TRUE), 2),
      Max = round(max(Value, na.rm = TRUE), 2),
      .groups = "drop"
    ) %>%
    kable(caption = caption) %>%
    kable_styling(
      bootstrap_options = c(
        "striped",
        "hover",
        "condensed"
      ),
      full_width = FALSE
    )
}
Code 
# Demographic fields

create_descriptive_table(
  df,
  c(
    "age",
    "height_cm",
    "weight_kg",
    "bmi"
  ),
  "Demographic Characteristics"
)

Missing Values

Code 
# Investigating missing values

missing_values <- df %>%
  summarise(across(everything(), ~sum(is.na(.)))) %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = everything(),
    names_to = "Variable",
    values_to = "Missing_Values"
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    Percent_Missing = round(100 * Missing_Values / nrow(df), 2)
  ) %>%
  filter(
    Percent_Missing > 0
  ) %>%
  arrange(desc(Missing_Values)) %>%
  mutate(
    Missing_Values = scales::comma(Missing_Values)
  )

missing_values %>%
  kable(caption = "Missing Values by Variable") %>%
  kable_styling(
    bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed"),
    full_width = FALSE
  )

Code 
# Count rows containing at least one missing value

rows_with_missing <- sum(!complete.cases(df))

# Calculate percentage of dataset removed

pct_rows_removed <- round(
  100 * rows_with_missing / nrow(df),
  2
)

missing_summary <- tibble(
  Total_Observations = scales::comma(nrow(df)),
  Rows_With_Missing = scales::comma(rows_with_missing),
  Percent_Removed = paste0(pct_rows_removed, "%")
)

missing_summary %>%
  kable(
    caption = "Impact of Missing Data Removal"
  ) %>%
  kable_styling(
    bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed"),
    full_width = FALSE
  )
Impact of Missing Data Removal
Total_Observations Rows_With_Missing Percent_Removed
55,200 1,057 1.91%

Categorical Fields

Code 
# Function to graph frequencies of categorical data

plot_categorical <- function(data, column) {

  plot_data <- data %>%
    count({{ column }}) %>%
    mutate(
      percent = round(100 * n / sum(n), 1),
      label = paste0(
        scales::comma(n),
        "\n(",
        percent,
        "%)"
      )
    )

  ggplot(
    plot_data,
    aes(
      x = reorder(as.character({{ column }}), -n),
      y = n
    )
  ) +
    geom_col() +
    geom_text(
      aes(label = label),
      vjust = -0.3,
      size = 3.5
    ) +
    scale_y_continuous(
      expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.15))
    ) +
    labs(
      title = paste(
        "Frequency of",
        tools::toTitleCase(
          gsub("_", " ", deparse(substitute(column)))
        )
      ),
      x = NULL,
      y = "Count"
    ) +
    theme_minimal() +
    theme(
      plot.title = element_text(
        face = "bold",
        hjust = 0.5
      ),
      axis.text.x = element_text(
        angle = 30,
        hjust = 1
      )
    )
}
Code 
# Apply graphing function

plot_categorical(df, mood)
Code 
plot_categorical(df, gender)
Code 
plot_categorical(df, region)
Code 
plot_categorical(df, workout_type)
Code 
plot_categorical(df, device_model)

Stress Distribution

Code 
# Investigating distribution of stress score within the dataset

ggplot(df, aes(x = stress_score)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 20) +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution of Stress Scores",
    subtitle = "Stress scores appear approximately normally distributed",
    x = "Stress Score",
    y = "Count"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

Modeling

  • Explain your data preprocessing and cleaning steps.
Code 
# Remove null values since there aren't many
df_clean <- na.omit(df)

# Convert categorical data to factor
df_clean <- df_clean %>%
  mutate(
    user_id = as.factor(user_id),
    gender = as.factor(gender),
    region = as.factor(region),
    device_model = as.factor(device_model),
    workout_type = as.factor(workout_type),
    mood = as.factor(mood)
  )

# Select all categorical variables
categorical_vars <- df_clean %>%
  select(
    user_id,
    gender,
    region,
    device_model,
    workout_type,
    mood
  )

# Select all continuous variables
continuous_vars <- df_clean %>%
  select(
    age, height_cm, weight_kg, bmi, resting_hr_bpm,
    avg_hr_day_bpm, hrv_rmssd_ms, spo2_avg_pct, sbp_mmHg,
    dbp_mmHg, steps, distance_km, calories_kcal,
    workout_minutes, caffeine_mg, alcohol_units, screen_time_min, 
    sleep_duration_hours, sleep_efficiency, sleep_latency_min,
    wake_after_sleep_onset_min, sleep_stage_rem_pct, sleep_stage_deep_pct, 
    sleep_stage_light_pct, stress_score, mindfulness_minutes
  )
Code 
# Calculate correlation matrix for continuous variables
# Helps identify correlated variables before building model
corr_matrix <- cor(
  continuous_vars,
  use = "complete.obs"
)

# Correlation heatmap
corrplot(
  corr_matrix,
  method = "color",
  type = "upper",
  tl.cex = 0.7
)
Code 
# Create table from the corr matrix
corr_table <- as.data.frame(as.table(corr_matrix)) %>%
  rename(
    Variable1 = Var1,
    Variable2 = Var2,
    Correlation = Freq
  ) %>%
  filter(
    as.character(Variable1) < as.character(Variable2)
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    abs_corr = abs(Correlation)
  ) %>%
  arrange(desc(abs_corr))

# Filter to only the highly correlated pairs and remove duplicate pairs
corr_table %>%
  filter(abs_corr >= 0.70) %>%
  mutate(
    Correlation = round(Correlation, 2)
  ) %>%
  select(
    Variable1,
    Variable2,
    Correlation
  ) %>%
  kable(
    caption = "Strong correlations among predictor variables"
  ) %>%
  kable_styling(full_width = FALSE)
Strong correlations among predictor variables
Variable1 Variable2 Correlation
distance_km steps 0.98
sleep_efficiency steps 0.83
sleep_stage_deep_pct sleep_stage_light_pct -0.83
distance_km sleep_efficiency 0.82
bmi weight_kg 0.81
avg_hr_day_bpm resting_hr_bpm 0.79
sleep_stage_deep_pct steps 0.77
distance_km sleep_stage_deep_pct 0.75
Code 
# Build initial model with all variables (except ones with strong correlations)
# Put user_id as the random effect

lmm_full <- lmer(
  stress_score ~
    age +
    gender +
    region +
    device_model +
    workout_type +
    bmi +
    resting_hr_bpm +
    hrv_rmssd_ms +
    spo2_avg_pct +
    sbp_mmHg +
    dbp_mmHg +
    distance_km +
    workout_minutes +
    caffeine_mg +
    alcohol_units +
    screen_time_min +
    mindfulness_minutes +
    sleep_duration_hours +
    sleep_efficiency +
    sleep_latency_min +
    wake_after_sleep_onset_min +
    (1 | user_id),
  data = df_clean
)

summary(lmm_full)
Linear mixed model fit by REML. t-tests use Satterthwaite's method [
lmerModLmerTest]
Formula: stress_score ~ age + gender + region + device_model + workout_type +  
    bmi + resting_hr_bpm + hrv_rmssd_ms + spo2_avg_pct + sbp_mmHg +  
    dbp_mmHg + distance_km + workout_minutes + caffeine_mg +  
    alcohol_units + screen_time_min + mindfulness_minutes + sleep_duration_hours +  
    sleep_efficiency + sleep_latency_min + wake_after_sleep_onset_min +  
    (1 | user_id)
   Data: df_clean

Some predictor variables are on very different scales: consider rescaling. 
You may also use (g)lmerControl(autoscale = TRUE) to improve numerical stability.
Code 
# Test mutlicollinearity using VIF

vif_full <- vif(
  lm(
    stress_score ~
      age +
      gender +
      region +
      device_model +
      workout_type +
      bmi +
      resting_hr_bpm +
      hrv_rmssd_ms +
      spo2_avg_pct +
      sbp_mmHg +
      dbp_mmHg +
      distance_km +
      workout_minutes +
      caffeine_mg +
      alcohol_units +
      screen_time_min +
      mindfulness_minutes +
      sleep_duration_hours +
      sleep_efficiency +
      sleep_latency_min +
      wake_after_sleep_onset_min,
    data = df_clean
  )
)

vif_full

Code 
# Test model after taking out region and device_model because they were not meaningful

lmm_no_region_device <- lmer(
  stress_score ~
    age +
    gender +
    workout_type +
    bmi +
    resting_hr_bpm +
    hrv_rmssd_ms +
    spo2_avg_pct +
    sbp_mmHg +
    dbp_mmHg +
    distance_km +
    workout_minutes +
    caffeine_mg +
    alcohol_units +
    screen_time_min +
    mindfulness_minutes +
    sleep_duration_hours +
    sleep_efficiency +
    sleep_latency_min +
    wake_after_sleep_onset_min +
    (1 | user_id),
  data = df_clean
)

summary(lmm_no_region_device)

Code 
# Reduced model version 1 after getting rid of irrelevant variables
# Variables with weak effects and little explanatory value were removed

lmm_reduced <- lmer(
  stress_score ~
    workout_type +
    hrv_rmssd_ms +
    distance_km +
    caffeine_mg +
    alcohol_units +
    screen_time_min +
    mindfulness_minutes +
    sleep_duration_hours +
    sleep_efficiency +
    wake_after_sleep_onset_min +
    (1 | user_id),
  data = df_clean
)

summary(lmm_reduced)

lmerModLmerTest]

Code 
# Test taking out workout_type to see if it contributes to model fit
lmm_reduced_2 <- lmer(
  stress_score ~
    hrv_rmssd_ms +
    distance_km +
    caffeine_mg +
    alcohol_units +
    screen_time_min +
    mindfulness_minutes +
    sleep_duration_hours +
    sleep_efficiency +
    wake_after_sleep_onset_min +
    (1 | user_id),
  data = df_clean
)

# Refit both models with maximum likelihood estimation (ML)
lmm_reduced_ml <- update(
  lmm_reduced,
  REML = FALSE
)

lmm_reduced_2_ml <- update(
  lmm_reduced_2,
  REML = FALSE
)

# Run anova
anova(
  lmm_reduced_2_ml,
  lmm_reduced_ml
)
Data: df_clean
Models:
lmm_reduced_2_ml: stress_score ~ hrv_rmssd_ms + distance_km + caffeine_mg + alcohol_units + screen_time_min + mindfulness_minutes + sleep_duration_hours + sleep_efficiency + wake_after_sleep_onset_min + (1 | user_id)
lmm_reduced_ml: stress_score ~ workout_type + hrv_rmssd_ms + distance_km + caffeine_mg + alcohol_units + screen_time_min + mindfulness_minutes + sleep_duration_hours + sleep_efficiency + wake_after_sleep_onset_min + (1 | user_id)
                 npar    AIC    BIC  logLik -2*log(L)  Chisq Df Pr(>Chisq)   
lmm_reduced_2_ml   12 349592 349699 -174784    349568                        
lmm_reduced_ml     18 349582 349743 -174773    349546 21.625  6   0.001416 **

# Test removing wake_after_sleep_onset_min to see if it contributes to model fit

  stress_score ~
    workout_type +
    hrv_rmssd_ms +
    distance_km +
    caffeine_mg +
    alcohol_units +
    screen_time_min +
    mindfulness_minutes +
    sleep_duration_hours +
    sleep_efficiency +
    (1 | user_id),
  data = df_clean
)

  lmm_reduced_3,
  REML = FALSE
)

# Run anova
anova(
  lmm_reduced_3_ml,
  lmm_reduced_ml
)

Code 
# Build final model
# The final predictor set was determined through iterative model reduction

lmm_final <- lmer(
  stress_score ~
    workout_type +
    hrv_rmssd_ms +
    distance_km +
    caffeine_mg +
    alcohol_units +
    screen_time_min +
    mindfulness_minutes +
    sleep_duration_hours +
    sleep_efficiency +
    wake_after_sleep_onset_min +
    (1 | user_id),
  data = df_clean
)

Code 
# Evaluate model assumptions and overall fit
plot(lmm_final)
Code 
qqnorm(residuals(lmm_final))
qqline(residuals(lmm_final))
Code 
# The random intercepts should be approximately normally distributed
qqnorm(ranef(lmm_final)$user_id[,1])
qqline(ranef(lmm_final)$user_id[,1])
Code 
# Check model diagnostics
check_model(lmm_final, check = "qq")
Code 
check_model(lmm_final, check = "homogeneity")
Code 
check_model(lmm_final, check = "linearity")
Code 
check_model(lmm_final, check = "outliers")
Code 
# Create an 80/20 train-test split at the user level
# Splitting by user prevents observations from the same participant appearing in both datasets

set.seed(1021)

users <- unique(df_clean$user_id)

train_users <- sample(
  users,
  size = round(length(users) * 0.80)
)

# Assign observations from training users to the training dataset
train_data <- df_clean %>%
  filter(user_id %in% train_users)

# Assign observations from held-out users to the testing dataset
test_data <- df_clean %>%
  filter(!user_id %in% train_users)

# Verify the number of unique users in each partition
length(unique(train_data$user_id))
[1] 240
Code 
length(unique(test_data$user_id))
[1] 60
Code 
# Verify the number of observations in each partition
nrow(train_data)
[1] 43311
Code 
nrow(test_data)
[1] 10832
Code 
# Check to make sure there are only unique users in train and test split
length(
  intersect(
    unique(train_data$user_id),
    unique(test_data$user_id)
  )
)
[1] 0
Code 
# Use the final model from before and build it from train dataset
lmm_train <- lmer(
  stress_score ~
    workout_type +
    hrv_rmssd_ms +
    distance_km +
    caffeine_mg +
    alcohol_units +
    screen_time_min +
    mindfulness_minutes +
    sleep_duration_hours +
    sleep_efficiency +
    wake_after_sleep_onset_min +
    (1 | user_id),
  data = train_data
)
Code 
# Predict on our test data
test_pred <- predict(
  lmm_train,
  newdata = test_data,
  allow.new.levels = TRUE
)
Code 
# Evaluate predictive performance on the test dataset

actual <- test_data$stress_score

rmse <- sqrt(mean((actual - test_pred)^2))

mae <- mean(abs(actual - test_pred))

r2_test <- cor(actual, test_pred)^2

Results

  • Present your key findings in a clear and concise manner.

  • Use visuals to support your claims.

  • Tell a story about what the data reveals.

Add results discussion here

Code 
# Plot predicted vs actual stress score to measure performance of model
ggplot(
  data.frame(actual, test_pred),
  aes(x = actual, y = test_pred)
) +
  geom_point(
    alpha = 0.05,
    size = 1
  ) +
  geom_abline(
    slope = 1,
    intercept = 0,
    linetype = "dashed"
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Predicted vs Actual Stress Scores",
    x = "Actual Stress Score",
    y = "Predicted Stress Score"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
Code 
# This table summarizes the statistical significance of each predictor retained in the final model

coef_table <- broom.mixed::tidy(lmm_final)

coef_table %>%
  filter(effect == "fixed") %>%
  select(
    term,
    estimate,
    std.error,
    statistic,
    p.value
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    across(
      c(estimate, std.error, statistic),
      ~ round(.x, 3)
    ),
    p.value = ifelse(
      p.value < .001,
      "<0.001",
      round(p.value, 3)
    )
  ) %>%
  kable(
    caption = "Fixed Effects from Final Linear Mixed Model"
  ) %>%
  kable_styling(
    bootstrap_options = c(
      "striped",
      "hover",
      "condensed"
    )
  )
Fixed Effects from Final Linear Mixed Model
term estimate std.error statistic p.value
(Intercept) 116.118 0.487 238.426 <0.001
workout_typemixed -0.135 0.150 -0.898 0.369
workout_typenone 0.084 0.090 0.926 0.354
workout_typerun -0.195 0.100 -1.957 0.05
workout_typestrength 0.093 0.107 0.875 0.382
workout_typewalk -0.169 0.097 -1.735 0.083
workout_typeyoga 0.153 0.120 1.279 0.201
hrv_rmssd_ms -0.258 0.003 -101.420 <0.001
distance_km -0.442 0.016 -28.136 <0.001
caffeine_mg 0.040 0.000 91.243 <0.001
alcohol_units 4.577 0.087 52.756 <0.001
screen_time_min 0.032 0.000 89.453 <0.001
mindfulness_minutes -0.042 0.005 -9.011 <0.001
sleep_duration_hours -5.974 0.035 -171.911 <0.001
sleep_efficiency -9.564 0.527 -18.134 <0.001
wake_after_sleep_onset_min 0.005 0.003 1.983 0.047
Code 
# Calculate final model performance metrics

r2_vals <- r2(lmm_final)
icc_val <- icc(lmm_final)

performance_table <- tibble(
  Metric = c(
    "Marginal R²",
    "Conditional R²",
    "ICC",
    "AIC",
    "Users",
    "Observations"
  ),
  Value = c(
    round(r2_vals$R2_marginal, 3),
    round(r2_vals$R2_conditional, 3),
    round(icc_val$ICC_adjusted, 3),
    round(AIC(lmm_final), 1),
    length(unique(df_clean$user_id)),
    nrow(df_clean)
  )
)

performance_table %>%
  kable(
    caption = "Final Model Performance Metrics"
  ) %>%
  kable_styling(
    bootstrap_options = c(
      "striped",
      "hover",
      "condensed"
    )
  )
Final Model Performance Metrics
Metric Value
Marginal R² 0.726
Conditional R² 0.729
ICC 0.013
AIC 349676.200
Users 300.000
Observations 54143.000
Code 
# Summarize predictive performance on the test dataset

prediction_table <- tibble(
  Metric = c(
    "Test R²",
    "RMSE",
    "MAE"
  ),
  Value = c(
    round(r2_test, 3),
    round(rmse, 3),
    round(mae, 3)
  )
)

prediction_table %>%
  kable(
    caption = "Prediction Performance on Test Data"
  ) %>%
  kable_styling(
    bootstrap_options = c(
      "striped",
      "hover",
      "condensed"
    )
  )
Prediction Performance on Test Data
Metric Value
Test R² 0.736
RMSE 6.128
MAE 4.882

References

Akarsu, Meftun. 2025. “Health + Wearables + Stress/Sleep Tracking (Synthetic).” Version 1. Kaggle. https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/mftnakrsu/health-wearables-stresssleep-tracking-syntc/data.
